headline: Core contracts are permissionless, but Uniswap Labs actively blocks screened addresses and tokens in its own interface

findings.text: The deployed PoolManager exposes core user actions such as unlock, initialize, modifyLiquidity, swap, donate, take, settle, settleFor, clear, mint, and burn without a whitelist/KYC modifier; the only privileged path found in the inspected core source is fee-controller administration in ProtocolFees, not user admission.

For the core function classes, pool creation is external, swaps and LP add/remove flow through unlock plus onchain pool or hook logic, and settlement/take/mint/burn are lock-scoped; I found no keeper, relayer, sequencer, or oracle-poster approval required to admit these actions.

Uniswap Labs documents active interface enforcement: unsupported-token pools have interactions and routing disabled, blocked addresses have state-changing actions disabled, and address screening uses TRM Labs intelligence.
The Terms of Service also contains passive eligibility and sanctions representations for app.uniswap.org, including age-of-majority and sanctions-status clauses.
Uniswap/interface releases publish official IPFS gateway mirrors of the Uniswap Interface, but those are official redistributions of the same Uniswap Labs interface rather than independent operators.

Independent access exists through direct deployed contracts and developer docs, the Etherscan Write Contract tab, and the separate cp0x-org permissionless Uniswap interface fork/live frontend; these paths are separate from app.uniswap.org.

I found no onchain sanctions or OFAC-style blocklist in the inspected PoolManager path; the documented sanctions/compliance tooling is applied by Uniswap Labs interfaces offchain through address screening and unsupported-token policy.

Read access is public through explorers/docs and the verified ABI, while write access to core PoolManager functions is available through contracts or periphery routes without protocol-level user allowlisting, subject to pool-specific onchain hook behavior.

The legal links were located on Uniswap support/terms pages and include verbatim eligibility/sanctions snippets; those clauses are policy restrictions, while the separate support policy and screening guide evidence active interface blocking.

steelman.red: The strongest red argument is that Uniswap Labs can actively disable state-changing actions for screened wallets and unsupported-token pools in its own products, which is a real admission restriction for users relying on the official interface.
steelman.orange: The strongest orange argument is that the canonical contracts admit users without whitelist/KYC or offchain approval, but the official Uniswap Labs frontend documents active TRM-based address screening and unsupported-token blocks.
steelman.green: The strongest green argument is that PoolManager itself is permissionless and non-upgradeable, with direct contract, SDK, explorer, and independent frontend access paths available outside app.uniswap.org.

rationale.verdict: Choosing orange because the smart contracts and direct paths are open, which rules out red, but Uniswap Labs' own support documentation says blocked addresses lose state-changing actions and unsupported-token pools have interactions/routing disabled, so the official interface has A3-active admission controls that prevent a green grade.

evidence.shows: Ethereum deployed PoolManager is source-verified, has Read Contract and Write Contract tabs, and its constructor initialOwner decodes to 0x1a9C8182C09F50C8318d769245beA52c32BE35BC.
PoolManager source defines unlock, initialize, modifyLiquidity, swap, donate, take, settle, settleFor, clear, mint, and burn with lock/no-delegatecall checks rather than allowlist/KYC checks.
ProtocolFees uses onlyOwner to set the protocolFeeController and restricts fee setting/collection to the controller; these are fee-admin functions, not user admission functions.
Canonical v4 deployment index lists PoolManager and periphery/router addresses across networks and is the deployed-contracts documentation page.
Docs describe PoolManager as the singleton entry point for v4 pool creation, swaps, liquidity management, hooks, and flash accounting.
ToS applies to app.uniswap.org and includes passive clauses: "age of majority in your jurisdiction" and "subject of economic or trade sanctions".
Uniswap Labs policy says interface blocks can disable unsupported-token pool interactions/routing and make blocked-address state-changing actions disabled.
Address screening guide says Uniswap Labs uses TRM Labs intelligence and blocks wallets linked to sanctions or other illegal activity from the web app/wallet.
Official app URL loaded as a JavaScript application shell during this run; no static HTTP block banner was returned in the fetched HTML.
Uniswap/interface releases publish IPFS CIDs and state that the latest release is mirrored at app.uniswap.org and accessible through IPFS gateways.
cp0x-org repository is a public fork from Uniswap/interface and describes itself as a permissionless Uniswap interface with Dockerfile and docker-compose files.
Live cp0x frontend URL returned a JavaScript app shell titled Permissionless Uniswap Frontend, separate from app.uniswap.org.
DexScreener independently surfaces Uniswap markets on Ethereum, including v4-labeled pairs and current volume/transaction data.
v4-core SECURITY.md points vulnerability disclosure to the Uniswap bug bounty and lists security@uniswap.org as a direct security contact.
Uniswap bug bounty redirects to a live Cantina bounty with maximum reward information and scope covering v4 core and related contracts.
Uniswap Governance forum identifies itself as the forum for governance proposal coordination and discussion.
Etherscan token page verifies Uniswap (UNI), symbol UNI, token contract 0x1f9840a85d5aF5bf1D1762F925BDADdC4201F984, and governance-token description.
Etherscan labels 0x1a9C8182C09F50C8318d769245beA52c32BE35BC as Uniswap V2: UNI Timelock.
Uniswap support describes v4 as a permissionless AMM using hooks, a singleton PoolManager, flash accounting, and non-upgradeable contracts.
Uniswap governance docs say UNI holders govern Uniswap and can control treasury spending, protocol fees, and UNI minting under governance.

unknowns: A3b: CoW Swap and 1inch candidate app pages rendered JavaScript shells or generic aggregator pages in this static fetch, so I did not cite them as verified Uniswap v4 access paths.

admin_addresses.role: PoolManager initialOwner and protocol fee controller setter

protocol_metadata.about: Uniswap v4 is a non-custodial AMM for token swaps and liquidity provision. It uses a singleton PoolManager for all pools plus flash accounting, and lets pool creators attach hooks that customize swap, fee, and LP behavior. Swappers trade against LP-funded pools, while liquidity providers create or manage positions and earn pool fees.

headline: Permissionless core contracts with robust third-party frontend alternatives

findings.text: The core PoolManager contract lacks any global whitelist or allowlist modifiers on primary user entry points such as unlock(), swap(), and modifyLiquidity(). These functions are marked as external and do not gate admission based on identity or address registration.
Uniswap V4 operates as a pull-based AMM; no off-chain keepers, sequencers, or relayers are required to admit or approve user transactions. Users interact directly with the PoolManager on-chain.

The official Uniswap Labs frontend (app.uniswap.org) actively enforces geo-blocking and wallet screening. The interface integrates TRM Labs to screen for sanctioned addresses and blocks users from jurisdictions including Iran, North Korea, and Syria.

Multiple independent access paths exist that are not bound by the Uniswap Labs ToS. These include the cp0x pi-uniswap-interface (uni.cp0x.com), which is open-source and self-hostable, and third-party aggregators like CowSwap and Matcha that route through Uniswap V4 contracts without Labs-imposed restrictions.
The core protocol contracts do not implement or reference any on-chain blocklists (e.g., OFAC) that would prevent specific addresses from interacting with the PoolManager.

Read access to pool state is entirely permissionless via public view functions. Write access (swapping, providing liquidity) is permissionless at the contract level, requiring only the provision of assets and execution of the unlock callback.

The Uniswap Labs ToS (Section 4.2) states: 'You represent and warrant that you are not... the subject of economic or trade sanctions... or located in, or a citizen or resident of, any state, country, territory or other jurisdiction that is sanctioned by the US or other applicable jurisdictions.'

steelman.red: While the core is permissionless, the 'hooks' architecture allows for the creation of pools that are entirely gated by a single entity, potentially fragmenting liquidity into permissioned silos.
steelman.orange: The primary and most user-friendly entry point (Uniswap Labs UI) actively screens and blocks users based on jurisdiction and wallet history, creating a significant barrier for non-technical users who cannot navigate alternative interfaces.
steelman.green: The protocol's core logic is immutable and permissionless, and the existence of numerous independent, open-source frontends ensures that no single entity can effectively gate access to the underlying liquidity.

rationale.verdict: Choosing green because the core PoolManager contract is entirely permissionless (A1, A4) and the active restrictions on the official frontend (A3-active) are mitigated by the high availability of independent, documented A3b-ii access paths like cp0x and direct contract interaction, which are standard for a protocol of this maturity.

evidence.shows: Public entry points unlock(), swap(), and modifyLiquidity() without whitelist modifiers.
Section 4.2 eligibility and sanctions clauses.
Independent, open-source frontend for Uniswap protocols.
Deployed PoolManager contract on Ethereum Mainnet.

protocol_metadata.about: Uniswap V4 is a decentralized exchange protocol that introduces a singleton architecture and 'hooks' to allow for highly customizable pool logic. It enables permissionless swapping and liquidity provision while reducing gas costs through multi-hop swap efficiency and transient storage.
